sentences.text: Well, my name is Cela Sarna-- C-E-L-A.
Sarna is S-A-R-N-A.
And I was born in Miedzyrzecz, Poland.
Should I spell it, Miedzyrzecz?
M-E-- M-I-E-N-D-Z-Y-R-Z-E-C, Poland.
My maiden name was Cyna, C-Y-N-A, Nisenbaum-- N-I-S-E-N-B-A-U-M.
I was born in 1924, the 22nd of November.
I didn't count it out yet.
[LAUGHTER] I think 73 or 74.
73, yeah.
Well, my mother was Freda, F-R-E-D-A, Feldbaum, F-E-L-D-B-A-U-M.
And I don't know her date of birth.
And my-- my father was Moyshe-- M-R-- M-O-Y-S-H-E-- Nisenbaum.
You had spelled it already.
And we were all in [?
Miedzyrzecz. ?] We were all in Miedzyrzecz.
And I had-- that's good.
I had seven sisters and one brother.
[CRYING]
Well, give me a minute.
I thought I would be better than I am.
Well--
Yeah.
Oh, they were much older, because I-- my father was in America for four or five years.
He only had two children.
And he made some money and sent it home, and we built a beautiful [? tree ?] family house.
And we all lived-- when I was born, I had already three sisters married and a brother.
So I don't remember their births or anything.
One was Esther, and gee-- and Leah and Breindel, and then was my brother, Yankel.
And then was my sister, Gitel and then Itkie, and Peskie, and I.
Two of them are alive.
They were in Russia.
When the war broke out, first came the Russian to our town.
And they were maybe two weeks.
And then they called us all.
We didn't have any radios or anything.
So they called us all into the middle of the city.
And they told us that they are going away, that the border between Russia and Germany will be Brest-Litovsk.
That's Brest [INAUDIBLE].
And we should all go with them, because the Germans will come in, and they'll kill us all out.
But we didn't believe them.
We all were in the house.
The war just broke out.
It was cozy.
We were all together.
All my sisters had three, four children.
One had five.
And where will you go?
We didn't have a lot of riches.
But whatever had-- we had was a treasure to my mother.
She lived in this house for so many years.
Where will she go?
Anyway, my two sisters went with the Russians.
They packed their belongings.
One was married, didn't have any children yet.
And one was not married yet.
And they both went.
They wanted to take me along.
But my mother didn't trust me with them.
And I was left at home.
I was--
This house?
Well, we lived in three rooms and a kitchen, which it was very, very lavish in Poland.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Excuse me.
And my father worked with my brother together.
And they were making [? brushes. ?] This was actually-- the-- the whole town did this.
And it was not bad.
We made a living.
Of course, whatever was living there is very poor here.
And my mother sent me to a private school, a folk school.
That's a Jewish school, a private.
Well, I started out-- I have pictures on that.
When I started out, I was five.
I went to the school until I was eight.
The reason she took me out, simple-- she couldn't afford to pay for me.
I hated the first of the month.
Every month, they-- they used to send me home for money.
And finally, I-- when I got a little older, I said, I don't want to go to this school.
I don't want to be sent home every-- every month for money.
I want to go to a public school.
And she took me out.
And she sent me to a public school.
And--
Yeah.
We had--
Well, it was a lot of beds [LAUGHS] because we are-- we were a lot of children in one room, and then a kitchen in a big-- they called this a saloon.
It was a big [INAUDIBLE], very nice.
I think my father send this from America.
I'm not sure, because it was very pretty.
And we had a nice dining room table.
Oh, and-- that's it.
This was in the-- in the thing.
No.
We had-- we had no electricity, no indoor plumbing, no toilets.
There was one toilet for maybe 10 families, where they lived all in one on this place.
And we all went-- what can I say?-- there to this toilet.
At night, if you needed, my mother had to go with me, because I was afraid to go out.
It was dark.
And that's how-- we were a middle-- let's say not poor, not rich, but comfortable.
Very lovable.
First of all, I was the youngest.
So they-- all the sisters loved me, because they already had children about my age-- very good parents.
If my-- I had girlfriends, where they were much richer than I am.
And then we-- Saturday, we wanted to go to see a movie, Shirley Temple or whatever it was.
It was $0.50, which it was a lot of money.
She didn't have it.
But she never let them go out from the house until she borrowed it from the next door neighbor-- I should go to the movie with them.
She was very, very lovable.
Very nice parents I had.
Well, the holidays and Shabbat, everybody was religious.
It wasn't such a thing that somebody didn't have any [? cows ?] or anything.
So everybody stayed home.
We used to make a cholent Friday and send it over to the baker.
And-- and Saturday, noontime, we used to take it out from the baker.
My mother used to pay for it Friday.
And then we needed tea.
So we went to one place a little bit further.
I mean, this was everything on the same street.
We lived on the nicest street in this town.
I used to go.
I used to go bring tea-- not the tea, just the water.
She cooked it.
And we paid ahead of time.
And then we bring-- we brought [INAUDIBLE].
And we had tea with the cholent, with some meat.
One day, she sent me for the cholent.
I'll tell you a-- a funny story.
And old cholent looked the same way, because they were-- my mother already recognized her-- her thing, what she put it into.
And I brought home, and my mother looked at it, and she said, oh, my god.
That's not mine.
And she took it fast back and brought ours.
It was some meat and potatoes, that's all.
No.
No.
Yiddish.
Yiddish.
They lived in the same town.
But they got married.
They lived in another town.
But when they got married, my mother had-- I told you, was a [? tree ?] family.
And since she had a lot of children, so she allowed them for three years to live in our house.
And when another one's supposed to get married, they moved on their own.
I actually was just at one wedding, because by the-- yeah.
And it was-- yeah, it was a small wedding, made in the house.
We all-- it wasn't like here, but it was a nice wedding.
Klezmers were playing.
And-- and we-- they danced all night.
This was the only sister was married what I remember.
And there was another wedding, when she married in 1939.
And already, Hitler started coming, [INAUDIBLE].
They didn't come to Poland yet.
But they send out all the Germans from-- if they had families-- and the war already was boiling.
So she got married.
But it was a very private wedding, in-- in the house, with just their family and our family and the rabbi.
Yeah.
Well, the public school was a public school.
I went into the second grade right away because I already learned in this school.
And I went every day to school.
And I came back 4 o'clock.
And on the way back, I used to go to learn Hebrew.
Well, this wasn't a lot of money.
I think it was one zloty for all week.
And I used-- when I came in on Friday, I brought the zloty.
I didn't like the school, really.
And I didn't want to go.
So one day--
No, this was a-- it was not a cheder, because a cheder, they called the-- for the boys.
It was for the girls.
And I want to just say it, and it just doesn't come to me.
All right, maybe later, it will come.
Anyway, to this school, I went.
And I really didn't like it.
And I didn't want to go.
Well, it's going to be hard to explain it now.
One day, she sat me on her knee, and she said to me-- I said, I won't go tomorrow.
She said to me, you know what?
I'll tell you something.
I'm an older woman, and some day I'll die.
I said and-- she said, if you don't say [YIDDISH].
I won't be able to go to heaven.
She said it in Yiddish.
And-- and if you-- and if you won't go to the school, you won't learn how to say [YIDDISH].
And with that-- this thought, I went every time we had to go, and I learned very well.
I still want to remember the school, and I can't remember.
Anyway, this was-- so I went to a public school.
And I went to this school to learn Hebrew.
And I came home.
At night, we had supper.
It wasn't a lot of children then at home, because just my sisters-- the ones where they lived through the war and me, because the others were married.
Yeah.
We had only Jewish friends.
Oh, more non-Jews than Jews, because this was a public school.
In school, was fine, but they lived in different parts of the town, and I never saw them.
But on Saturday, all the Jews got together.
All my friends are Jewish.
We got together, went to the movies.
We belonged to a Zionist organization.
So we went dancing, [INAUDIBLE], and things like that.
Yeah.
It was [INAUDIBLE].
You see, in our town was 90-- 80% Jews.
So really-- I hear a lot of people talking, but really, I-- in our place where we lived, I never saw anything or heard anything, really not.
In 1939, when-- really, before-- a couple months before the war.
Oh, it was very well observed.
Well, two weeks before, we started making matzah.
We-- it was in private homes.
There were people working.
And they were preparing the matzah and baking them.
And my mother used to go in and buy whatever she needed.
I think they bought it in a big-- I don't know how many pounds, really.
But they brought it home.
And we had it over the Pesach.
She-- she cleaned.
The whole the house was empty.
They took out the beds.
And they-- and they cleaned the beds, and they washed the floors.
And they-- they painted the walls with white and took out all the [YIDDISH], because we had a lot.
And our whole [INAUDIBLE] was outside.
It was a real preparation.
I hated this.
Before really came the holiday, she was so tired that she couldn't wait until it's over.
It was a big preparation.
We had then the Seder.
All the kids came to our house.
And we all-- always had my father with all the children, was sitting ahead of the table, and we had the Seder.
I was the youngest.
I always I am ashamed to say it, but I never was till the end.
I always fell asleep.
My mother used to put me to bed.
No.
We-- we had younger kids.
My sisters' younger children, they were saying the four questions.
It really came out in one time.
The war came out, and it's-- from day to day, we had different things.
The first thing was-- well, we felt it right away, because in a month, the Germans took over the whole street, because, I said, it was the nicest street.
And they threw us out.
They gave us only three hours to pack the bags and go out.
This was in '39, about December.
It was very cold.
And my-- the older brothers and the brother and all their brother-in-laws came.
They helped us pack, because they lived in a different-- a different part of the town.
And they-- they were not relocated.
And we packed whatever we could.
And they found us a place.
It was horrible and it-- on the second floor, a walk up, with a little kitchen and just one room.
No furniture.
No, we left everything.
Just the beds we took.
And-- because it-- this place where we went to was a place just to put two beds, really.
And we moved there.
And-- and from then on, we already started feeling the war, because [INAUDIBLE]-- when you are not in your own home, you already, you are a refugee right way.
And the place where we moved was by the big shul.
They converted the shul to a displaced person place.
And they started bringing people from the smaller towns into our town.
And they located them in this big shul.
We had a beautiful shul.
No, we really went to a shtiebel, where it was-- it was smaller.
It wasn't a [INAUDIBLE].
It was a nice synagogue.
But that was on the same street where we lived.
Well, they made-- they took all the homes, and they made it for a commandant too for the Germans-- not only my house, it was hundreds of houses. 100 houses on this place, on this street.
The whole street, nobody could go in.
I-- later, we went in, because we went every day to-- to clean for the Germans.
And--
No, I never went as far as my-- my house, no.
No, I don't remember the rabbi's name.
But my mother used to go with me Saturdays, used to take me to the-- to the synagogue.
My mother was well educated in Yiddish and in Polish.
And-- and it was very seldom in Europe that the people were educated in that.
So my mother used to sit in the middle.
You know, now, I remember the thing.
In Bais Yaakov, I went.
Now, it came.
So she sat in the middle with the [YIDDISH], they called it in Yiddish.
And all the women were sitting around.
And I sat on her knees.
And she was davening.
An older woman was sitting here crying, and I hated it.
I really didn't want to see my mother crying and all the other women crying.
But she took me for a while until I grow up a little older, and I didn't want to go anymore.

Well, because if you know what you're saying, you pray to God, they should be healthy, they should marry all of their children and have some money, you know-- and people were very religious years ago, not religious what it was good for them, but really religious.
They fanatic.
They believed in it-- what they were saying, you know?
Yes.
The war was already in.
My mother used to go every day-- not every day, every second day-- to stay half-- a half a night in the-- in a line for a piece of bread.
Right away, we didn't have nothing to eat, because we had only the rations, what they gave us.
It's a funny part that we-- you ask me about anti-Semite.
We couldn't walk on the sidewalk.
Right away, we had yellow stars in a yellow band on the-- on the arm, on the right side.
This was right away in the end of 1939.
And when we had the rations, we didn't have enough to eat.
My mother used to stay in line.
And then I figured, why should she stay in the line?
I'll go.
And I used to stay every day in the line.
Not ?] once, I stayed a half a night, and when it came to me, they said that they don't have any more bread.
In 19-- the beginning of '40, I think it was like in January-- it was on a Tuesday.
I stayed in the line and I came in.
And my mother was in bed.
And all of a sudden, she woke up, and she says, oh, I forgot to [YIDDISH].
I said, no, today is Tuesday.
And I realized right away that she caught the typhus fever, because this was going around.
People were dying like the flies.
From the synagogue, what I said, from this shul, they used to take out 20 a day, 30 a day, because this is very, very contagious.
If once has it-- one has it in the family, the whole family gets it.
What should I do?
So I ran.
One thing I'll tell you, before I start out-- when the war out-- was out, I was a child, but I grew up in one year, I think 30 years.
I knew how to take care of myself.
I knew how to be wise.
I knew when-- when to do anything.
I grew up very, very fast.
I was, that time, already, I think, 14.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Anyway, I went to the doctor, and the doctor came up.
He knew us very well.
He was a neighbor of ours when we lived on this-- yet in our own-- own street.
He came up, and he said, she has typhus fever.
He said, the best thing, to take her to the hospital.
That time was yet a Jewish hospital.
We took her to the hospital.
And typhus fever has like a-- a date-- seven days later.
You-- oh, you turned better, or you die.
It's like a crisis.
If you live through the crisis, you live.
Anyway, she lived through the crisis.
And we took her home.
Yeah.
I forgot to say that my father had typhus fever in the First World War.
So this, you can have only once in your life.
And he was the one-- he took her to the hospital on his back in his-- and he really [? worked ?] [? around with ?] all of us.
When she was in the hospital, I got it with my sister.
We went through.
But we already were in the house, because he-- he saw that they-- they don't take care of, really, of the people.
So he didn't trust them.
And he took care of us.
And we were sick at home.
And my mother came home after two weeks.
She was very weak.
Do you remember approximately how old she was at that point?
She was 60.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] [SIGHS] And-- and we lived through this.
And then in 19-- well, they took us to work every day.
And really, they-- they took-- my father shaved off the beard.
Otherwise, the Germans shaved it off if they saw it.
So they shaved it off with-- with flesh.
He did it himself.
No.
Right away, school started-- well, I graduated, really, school then, because when we were 13, we graduated school-- grammar school.
We went seven years.
And we had to go to work.
My father was older than my mother yet.
So we didn't want the father was-- to go to school-- to work.
So I really worked every day.
One day, I went with my [GERMAN].
And one day I went with his [GERMAN].
And he stayed home.
He stayed home, and I used to clean by the Germans.
I used to wash the floors.
And that's what we did.
We didn't clean them.
We washed the floors.
This was my occupation.
Or we-- we went to a-- to a kitchen where they cooked for the Germans, and we peeled potatoes, carrots, prepared everything.
I don't-- no.
Only me and my other sister, when she was still not married and she stayed with us.
For all the time until I got relocated.
[SIGHS]
